title: Noun

infobox.value: is a word that represents a concrete or abstract thing, like living creatures, places, actions, qualities, states of existence, and ideas. To summarize this, Nouns signify an object or idea. A noun may serve as an [object](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)") or [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") within a [phrase](/wiki/Phrase "Phrase"), [clause](/wiki/Clause "Clause"), or [sentence](/wiki/Sentence_(linguistics) "Sentence (linguistics)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[note 1]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Part of speech that names an object or set of objects

For other uses, see [Noun (disambiguation)](/wiki/Noun_(disambiguation) "Noun (disambiguation)").

In [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar"), a **noun** is a word that represents a concrete or abstract thing, like living creatures, places, actions, qualities, states of existence, and ideas. To summarize this, Nouns signify an object or idea. A noun may serve as an [object](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)") or [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") within a [phrase](/wiki/Phrase "Phrase"), [clause](/wiki/Clause "Clause"), or [sentence](/wiki/Sentence_(linguistics) "Sentence (linguistics)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[note 1]](#cite_note-2)

In [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), nouns constitute a lexical category ([part of speech](/wiki/Part_of_speech "Part of speech")) defined according to how its members combine with members of other lexical categories. The [syntactic](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") occurrence of nouns differs among languages.

In English, prototypical nouns are [common nouns or proper nouns](/wiki/Proper_noun "Proper noun") that can occur with [determiners](/wiki/Determiners "Determiners"), [articles](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") and [attributive adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"), and can function as the [head](/wiki/Head_(linguistics) "Head (linguistics)") of a [noun phrase](/wiki/Noun_phrase "Noun phrase"). According to traditional and popular classification, [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") are distinct from nouns, but in much modern theory they are considered a subclass of nouns.[[2]](#cite_note-3) Every language has various linguistic and grammatical distinctions between nouns and [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb").[[3]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also [History of parts of speech](/wiki/Part_of_speech#History "Part of speech")

[Word classes](/wiki/Word_class "Word class") (parts of speech) were described by [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") grammarians from at least the 5th century BC. In [Yāska](/wiki/Y%C4%81ska "Yāska")'s *[Nirukta](/wiki/Nirukta "Nirukta")*, the noun (*nāma*) is one of the four main categories of words defined.[[4]](#cite_note-Matilal-5)

The [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") equivalent was *ónoma* (ὄνομα), referred to by [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") in the [*Cratylus*](/wiki/Cratylus_(dialogue) "Cratylus (dialogue)"), and later listed as one of the eight parts of speech in *[The Art of Grammar](/wiki/The_Art_of_Grammar "The Art of Grammar")*, attributed to [Dionysius Thrax](/wiki/Dionysius_Thrax "Dionysius Thrax") (2nd century BC). The term used in [Latin grammar](/wiki/Latin_grammar "Latin grammar") was *nōmen*. All of these terms for "noun" were also words meaning "name".[[5]](#cite_note-6) The English word *noun* is derived from the Latin term, through the [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language") *nom* (other forms include *nomme*, and *noun* itself).

The word classes were defined partly by the grammatical [forms](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") that they take. In Sanskrit, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), and Latin, for example, nouns are categorized by [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") and inflected for [case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") and [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"). Because [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") share these three [grammatical categories](/wiki/Grammatical_category "Grammatical category"), adjectives typically were placed in the same class as nouns.

Similarly, the Latin term *nōmen* includes both nouns (substantives) and adjectives, as originally did the English word *noun*, the two types being distinguished as *nouns substantive* and *nouns adjective* (or *substantive nouns* and *adjective nouns*, or simply *substantives* and *adjectives*). (The word *[nominal](/wiki/Nominal_(linguistics) "Nominal (linguistics)")* is now sometimes used to denote a class that includes both nouns and adjectives.)

Many European languages use a [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") of the word *substantive* as the basic term for *noun* (for example, Spanish *sustantivo*, "noun"). Nouns in the dictionaries of such languages are demarked by the abbreviation *s.* or *sb.* instead of *n.*, which may be used for proper nouns or neuter nouns instead. In English, some modern authors use the word *substantive* to refer to a class that includes both nouns (single words) and [noun phrases](/wiki/Noun_phrase "Noun phrase") (multiword units that are sometimes called *noun equivalents*).[[6]](#cite_note-CMOS-7) It can also be used as a counterpart to *attributive* when distinguishing between a noun being used as the [head](/wiki/Head_(linguistics) "Head (linguistics)") (main word) of a [Noun phrase](/wiki/Noun_phrase "Noun phrase") and a noun being used as a [noun adjunct](/wiki/Noun_adjunct "Noun adjunct"). For example, the noun *knee* can be said to be used substantively in *my knee hurts*, but attributively in *the patient needed knee replacement*.

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Examples")]

* The *cat* sat on the *chair*.
* Please hand in your *assignments* by the *end* of the *week*.
* *Cleanliness* is next to *godliness*.
* *Plato* was an influential *philosopher* in ancient *Greece*.
* Revel the *night*, rob, murder, and commit / The oldest *sins* the newest *kind* of *ways*? Henry IV Part 2, act 4 scene 5.

A noun can co-occur with an [article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") or an [attributive adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"). Verbs and adjectives cannot. In the following, an asterisk (\*) in front of an example means that this example is ungrammatical.

* *the name* (*name* is a noun: can co-occur with a definite article *the*)
* *\*the baptise* (*baptise* is a verb: cannot co-occur with a definite article)
* *constant circulation* (*circulation* is a noun: can co-occur with the attributive adjective *constant*)
* *\*constant circulate* (*circulate* is a verb: cannot co-occur with the attributive adjective *constant*)
* *a fright* (*fright* is a noun: can co-occur with the indefinite article *a*)
* *\*an afraid* (*afraid* is an adjective: cannot co-occur with the article *a*)
* *terrible fright* (the noun *fright* can co-occur with the adjective *terrible*)
* *\*terrible afraid* (the adjective *afraid* cannot co-occur with the adjective *terrible*)

## Characterization and definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Characterization and definition")]

Nouns have sometimes been characterized in terms of the [grammatical categories](/wiki/Grammatical_category "Grammatical category") by which they may be varied (for example [gender](/wiki/Gender_(linguistics) "Gender (linguistics)"), [case](/wiki/Case_(linguistics) "Case (linguistics)"), and [number](/wiki/Number_(linguistics) "Number (linguistics)")). Such definitions tend to be language-specific, since different languages may apply different categories.

Nouns are frequently defined, particularly in informal contexts, in terms of their [semantic](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") properties (their meanings). Nouns are described as words that refer to a *person*, *place*, *thing*, *event*, *substance*, *quality*, *quantity*, etc., but this manner of definition has been criticized as uninformative.[[7]](#cite_note-jackendoff-8)

Several English nouns lack an intrinsic [referent](/wiki/Referent "Referent") of their own: *behalf* (as in *on behalf of*), *dint* (*by dint of*), and *sake* (*for the sake of*).[[8]](#cite_note-9) Moreover, other parts of speech may have reference-like properties: the verbs *to rain* or *to mother*, or adjectives like *red*; and there is little difference between the adverb *gleefully* and the [prepositional phrase](/wiki/Preposition_and_postposition "Preposition and postposition") *with glee*.[[note 2]](#cite_note-10)

A [functional](/wiki/Functional_linguistics "Functional linguistics") approach defines a noun as a word that can be the head of a nominal phrase, i.e., a phrase with referential function, without needing to go through morphological transformation.[[9]](#cite_note-rijkhoff2022-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classification")]

Nouns can have a number of different properties and are often sub-categorized based on various of these criteria, depending on their occurrence in a language. Nouns may be classified according to [morphological](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") properties such as which [prefixes](/wiki/Prefix "Prefix") or [suffixes](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") they take, and also their relations in [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") – how they combine with other words and expressions of various types.

Many such classifications are language-specific, given the obvious differences in syntax and morphology. In English for example, it might be noted that nouns are words that can co-occur with definite articles (as stated at the start of this article), but this could not apply in [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), which has no definite articles.

### Gender

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gender")]

Main article: [Grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender")

In some languages common and proper nouns have grammatical gender, typically masculine, feminine, and neuter. The gender of a noun (as well as its number and case, where applicable) will often require [agreement](/wiki/Agreement_(linguistics) "Agreement (linguistics)") in words that modify or are used along with it. In [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") for example, the singular form of the definite article is *le* for masculine nouns and *la* for feminine; adjectives and certain verb forms also change (sometimes with the simple addition of *-e* for feminine). Grammatical gender often correlates with the form of the noun and the inflection pattern it follows; for example, in both [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") most nouns ending in *-a* are feminine. Gender can also correlate with the [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex") or [social gender](/wiki/Social_gender "Social gender") of the noun's referent, particularly in the case of nouns denoting people (and sometimes animals), though with exceptions (the feminine French noun *personne* can refer to a male or a female person).

In Modern English, even common nouns like *hen* and *princess* and proper nouns like *Alicia* do not have grammatical gender (their femininity has no relevance in syntax), though they denote persons or animals of a specific sex. The gender of a pronoun must be appropriate for the item referred to: "The *girl* said the *ring* was from *her* new *boyfriend*, but *he* denied *it* was from *him*" (three nouns; and three gendered pronouns: or four, if this *her* is counted as a [possessive pronoun](/wiki/English_possessive#Pronouns "English possessive")).

### Proper and common nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Proper and common nouns")]

Main article: [Proper noun](/wiki/Proper_noun "Proper noun")

A *proper noun* (sometimes called a *proper name*, though the two terms normally have different meanings) is a noun that represents a unique entity (*India*, *[Pegasus](/wiki/Pegasus "Pegasus")*, *[Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")*, *[Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius")*, *[Pequod](/wiki/Pequod_(Moby-Dick) "Pequod (Moby-Dick)")*) – as distinguished from *common nouns* (or **appellative nouns**), which describe a class of entities (*country*, *animal*, *planet*, *person*, *ship*).[[11]](#cite_note-13) In Modern English, most proper nouns – unlike most common nouns – are capitalized regardless of context (*Albania*, *Newton*, *Pasteur*, *America*), as are many of the forms that are derived from them (the common noun in "he's an *Albanian*"; the adjectival forms in "he's of *Albanian* heritage" and "*Newtonian* physics", but not in "*pasteurized* milk"; the second verb in "they sought to *Americanize* us").

### Countable nouns and mass nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Countable nouns and mass nouns")]

Main articles: [Count noun](/wiki/Count_noun "Count noun") and [Mass noun](/wiki/Mass_noun "Mass noun")

*Count nouns* or *countable nouns* are common nouns that can take a [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural"), can combine with [numerals](/wiki/Numeral_system "Numeral system") or counting [quantifiers](/wiki/Quantifiers_(linguistics) "Quantifiers (linguistics)") (e.g., *one*, *two*, *several*, *every*, *most*), and can take an indefinite article such as *a* or *an* (in languages that have such articles). Examples of count nouns are *chair*, *nose*, and *occasion*.

*Mass nouns* or *uncountable* (*non-count*) *nouns* differ from count nouns in precisely that respect: they cannot take plurals or combine with number words or the above type of quantifiers. For example, the forms *a furniture* and *three furnitures* are not used – even though *pieces* of furniture can be counted. The distinction between mass and count nouns does not primarily concern their corresponding referents but more how the nouns *present* those entities.[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15)

Many nouns have both countable and uncountable uses; for example, *soda* is countable in "give me three sodas", but uncountable in "he likes soda".

### Collective nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Collective nouns")]

Main article: [Collective noun](/wiki/Collective_noun "Collective noun")

*Collective nouns* are nouns that – even when they are treated in their morphology and syntax as [singular](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") – refer to *groups* consisting of more than one individual or entity. Examples include *committee*, *government*, and *police*. In English these nouns may be followed by a singular or a plural verb and referred to by a singular or plural pronoun, the singular being generally preferred when referring to the body as a unit and the plural often being preferred, especially in British English, when emphasizing the individual members.[[14]](#cite_note-Gowers_2014-16) Examples of acceptable and unacceptable use given by Gowers in *Plain Words* include:[[14]](#cite_note-Gowers_2014-16)

"A committee *was* appointed to consider this subject." (singular)

"The committee *were* unable to agree." (plural)

\* "The committee were of one mind when I sat in on them." (unacceptable use of plural)

### Concrete nouns and abstract nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Concrete nouns and abstract nouns")]

Further information: [Physical body](/wiki/Physical_body "Physical body") and [Abstract object](/wiki/Abstract_object "Abstract object")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Noun "Special:EditPage/Noun") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

*Concrete nouns* refer to [physical entities](/wiki/Physical_bodies "Physical bodies") that can, in principle at least, be observed by at least one of the [senses](/wiki/Sense "Sense") (*chair*, *apple*, *Janet*, *atom*), as items supposed to exist in the physical world. *Abstract nouns*, on the other hand, refer to [abstract objects](/wiki/Abstract_object "Abstract object"): ideas or concepts (*justice*, *anger*, *solubility*, *duration*).

Some nouns have both concrete and abstract meanings: *art* usually refers to something abstract ("Art is important in human culture"), but it can also refer to a concrete item ("I put my daughter's art up on the fridge"). A noun might have a literal (concrete) and also a figurative (abstract) meaning: "a brass *key*" and "the *key* to success"; "a *block* in the pipe" and "a mental *block*". Similarly, some abstract nouns have developed etymologically by figurative extension from literal roots (*drawback*, *fraction*, *holdout*, *uptake*).

Many abstract nouns in English are formed by adding a suffix (*-ness*, *-ity*, *-ion*) to adjectives or verbs (*happiness* and *serenity* from the adjectives *happy* and *serene*; *circulation* from the verb *circulate*).

### Alienable vs. inalienable nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Alienable vs. inalienable nouns")]

Main article: [Inalienable possession](/wiki/Inalienable_possession "Inalienable possession")

Illustrating the wide range of possible classifying principles for nouns, the [Awa language](/wiki/Awa_language_(Papua_New_Guinea) "Awa language (Papua New Guinea)") of [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea")[[15]](#cite_note-17) regiments nouns according to how *ownership* is assigned: as alienable possession or [inalienable](/wiki/Inalienable_possession "Inalienable possession") possession. An alienably possessed item (a tree, for example) can exist even without a possessor. But inalienably possessed items are necessarily associated with their possessor and are referred to differently, for example with nouns that function as kin terms (meaning "father", etc.), body-part nouns (meaning "shadow", "hair", etc.), or part–whole nouns (meaning "top", "bottom", etc.).

## Noun phrases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Noun phrases")]

Main article: [Noun phrase](/wiki/Noun_phrase "Noun phrase")

A noun phrase (or **NP**) is a phrase usually [headed](/wiki/Head_(linguistics) "Head (linguistics)") by a common noun, a proper noun, or a pronoun. The head may be the only constituent, or it may be modified by [determiners](/wiki/Determiner "Determiner") and [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"). For example, "The dog sat near Ms Curtis and wagged its tail" contains three NPs: *the dog* (subject of the verbs *sat* and *wagged*); *Ms Curtis* (complement of the preposition *near*); and *its tail* (object of *wagged*). "You became their teacher" contains two NPs: *you* (subject of *became*); and *their teacher*.[[note 3]](#cite_note-18)

## Nouns in relation to other word classes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Nouns in relation to other word classes")]

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

Main article: [Pronoun](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun")

Nouns and noun phrases can typically be replaced by [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun"), such as *he, it, she, they, which, these*, and *those*, to avoid repetition or explicit identification, or for other reasons (but as noted earlier, current theory often classifies pronouns as a subclass of nouns parallel to *prototypical nouns*). For example, in the sentence "Gareth thought she was weird", the word *she* is a pronoun that refers to a person just as the noun *Gareth* does. The word *one* can replace parts of noun phrases, and it sometimes stands in for a noun. An example is given below:

John's car is newer than *the one* that Bill has.

But *one* can also stand in for larger parts of a noun phrase. For example, in the following example, *one* can stand in for *new car*.

This new car is cheaper than *that one*.

### Nominalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Nominalization")]

Main article: [Nominalization](/wiki/Nominalization "Nominalization")

Nominalization is a process whereby a word that belongs to another part of speech comes to be used as a noun. This can be a way to create new nouns, or to use other words in ways that resemble nouns. In French and Spanish, for example, adjectives frequently act as nouns referring to people who have the characteristics denoted by the adjective. This sometimes happens in English as well, as in the following examples:

This legislation will have the most impact on the *poor*.

The race is not to the *swift*, nor the battle to the *powerful*.

The Socialist *International* is a worldwide association of political parties.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noun&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Example nouns for:
   * Living creatures (including people, alive, dead, or imaginary): *mushrooms, dogs, [Afro-Caribbeans](/wiki/Afro-Caribbean "Afro-Caribbean"), rosebushes, [Mandela](/wiki/Mandela "Mandela"), bacteria, [Klingons](/wiki/Klingons "Klingons")*, etc.
   * Physical objects: *hammers, pencils, Earth, guitars, atoms, stones, boots, shadows*, etc.
   * Places: *closets, temples, rivers, Antarctica, houses, [Uluru](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru"), [utopia](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia")*, etc.
   * Actions of individuals or groups: *swimming, exercises, cough, explosions, flight, electrification, embezzlement*, etc.
   * Physical qualities: *colors, lengths, porosity, weights, roundness, symmetry, solidity,* etc.
   * Mental or bodily states: *jealousy, sleep, joy, headache, confusion*, etc.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-10)**  Idioms often include nouns in a way that may be independent of any nominal meaning they may have: in *rock and roll* there is no reference to any "rock" or any "roll"; *lock, stock, and barrel* is a [dead metaphor](/wiki/Dead_metaphor "Dead metaphor") that refers only to a figurative sense of a *lock* or *stock* or *barrel*. See [hendiadys](/wiki/Hendiadys "Hendiadys") and [hendiatris](/wiki/Hendiatris "Hendiatris").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** In this position *their teacher* would be analysed variously under different linguistic theories. For example, some would classify it as a "predicate nominal over the subject" (as in the article [Predicative expression](/wiki/Predicative_expression "Predicative expression")); but all would agree that it is not an object since *became* is not [transitive](/wiki/Transitive_verb "Transitive verb"). Traditionally, and very commonly in mainstream linguistic analysis, it is classified as a complement or *predicative complement* (PC); see extended treatment in Chapter 4 ("The clause: complements") of Huddleston and Pullum (2002), pp. 213–321: for example in §5.1 at p. 253, where the NP *a minister* is taken as a PC in "Ed became a minister" contrasting with its role as an object (O) in "Ed attacked a minister".
